turns.content: Hey, I'm back in the game after five years and trying to fix our CI/CD pipeline that's failing because it can't reach external APIs. I thought using reqwest with a simple string config for the proxy settings would be enough, so I wrote this: let client = reqwest::ClientBuilder::new().proxy(reqwest::Proxy::all("http://my-proxy.local:3128")).build();

That looks like a good start, but you're making a common mistake when you just pass a string. reqwest doesn't automatically parse that URL into the right internal structure for your proxy logic. You need to use the specific Proxy enum variant and then attach it via the builder method on the Client type instead of passing raw text directly.
Oh, I see. So I should be constructing a new Proxy object first? Like using reqwest::Proxy::from_url("http://my-proxy.local:3128") and then calling .proxy() on the builder?

Exactly, you're getting closer. But there's a nuance here for your corporate setup since it might require SOCKS5 or have specific NO_PROXY environment variables active. The reqwest client builder accepts either a direct Proxy struct instance or an Option<Proxy> depending on whether you want the proxy to apply globally or selectively. Since you mentioned corporate proxies, are you also setting up any special handling for localhost requests that should bypass the proxy using env vars?

I'm hitting a 502 error when my application calls an external API through our corporate proxy. The Java client works fine but reqwest refuses to connect even though I've set the proxy string correctly in the builder. Why is this happening?

The issue stems from how reqwest handles the proxy configuration compared to your Java experience. When you pass a proxy URL directly into reqwest::Client::builder().proxy(), it attempts to use that specific proxy regardless of system environment variables like HTTP_PROXY or HTTPS_PROXY. In contrast, many higher-level languages respect these env vars by default unless explicitly overridden. Your corporate setup likely relies on the NO_PROXY variable to exclude internal domains from being routed through the proxy. Since reqwest doesn't automatically check your environment for this exclusion list when you provide a hardcoded string, it tries to reach an internal host via the external proxy and fails at the gateway level.
That makes sense about the env vars but I'm confused on how to properly configure NO_PROXY in Rust code. Is there a trait or type that exposes this environment variable?

Rust does not provide a built-in trait specifically named 'ProxyEnv' for this purpose, so you cannot rely on automatic trait-based resolution like some other ecosystems might suggest. Instead, you must manually read the NO_PROXY environment variable using std::env::var() and parse it into a Vec<String>. Then, when constructing your reqwest client with a proxy URL, you can implement custom logic to check if the target host exists in that parsed list before attempting to use the proxy connection. This manual parsing is necessary because there is no standard library function or crate trait that automatically bridges std::env and the reqwest builder for this specific exclusion behavior.
Okay, so I need to write custom logic. But what if our corporate network uses SOCKS5 instead of HTTP proxy? Can reqwest handle that natively without extra crates?

No, reqwest does not have native support for SOCKS5 proxies; it only understands standard HTTP and HTTPS proxy headers like CONNECT. If your corporate infrastructure requires SOCKS5, you cannot use the built-in proxy configuration in reqwest because there is no trait or method that accepts a SOCKS5 URL string directly. You must implement a custom transport layer using tokio::net::TcpStream combined with the socks crate to handle the SOCKS5 handshake before passing the connection through to reqwest's HTTP client. This means you cannot simply configure the proxy in the builder; you must intercept the TCP stream and wrap it yourself.
I see, so I'd need to write a custom transport wrapper around TcpStream. Is there an existing crate that does this for me?

Yes, the socks crate provides a trait implementation called SocksProxyHandler that you can use to wrap your TcpStream and handle the SOCKS5 handshake automatically. By importing this crate and implementing reqwest's custom transport logic around it, you avoid reinventing the socket negotiation protocol yourself. However, be aware that even with this wrapper, you still cannot rely on automatic environment variable detection for NO_PROXY unless you manually parse those variables in your application code before initializing the connection.

Got it, I'll use socks crate and manually check env vars. One last question: if we have multiple proxies configured (HTTP vs SOCKS5) depending on destination, how do I dynamically choose which one to use?

You cannot configure conditional proxy selection based on the destination URL within reqwest's standard builder; there is no trait or method that accepts a predicate function for host routing. Instead, you must implement a custom transport layer that inspects the request before making the connection. You can write a middleware or wrapper around TcpStream that checks if the target hostname matches an internal domain (using the NO_PROXY list) and then chooses whether to pass the stream directly or route it through either your HTTP proxy string or SOCKS5 socket depending on the environment variable configuration at runtime.
Imagine I'm trying to fetch data from a corporate server but my local machine is behind a proxy, and suddenly the request fails with a timeout error. How does reqwest handle this situation?

When you are building a client using reqwest, you must configure the builder to set the HTTP proxy environment variable so that the crate can route traffic through your corporate gateway instead of trying to connect directly.

I tried setting the env var but it didn't work. I suspect my colleague might have made a mistake by forgetting to check if their machine is actually behind one, or perhaps they didn't set NO_PROXY correctly when needed.

Think of the proxy configuration like a traffic cop at an intersection; if you tell your car (the request) where to go but don't clear the side streets for emergency vehicles, the car will get stuck. You need to ensure reqwest knows which URLs should bypass the proxy by setting NO_PROXY correctly before calling the client builder.
